perturbation.original_text: If the security requirements for accessing, handling, and storing VA data and systems are specified in the contract, the contractor will comply with the contractual security requirements.
perturbation.changed_text: The contractor will handle the VA property.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the clause that explicitly states the contractor will comply with contractual security requirements if specified in the contract, a contradiction arises. Later sections outlining security protocols assume such compliance is mandatory, leading to uncertainty about enforcement if no specific requirements are present.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Contractor personnel performing work under this contract shall satisfy all requirements for appropriate security eligibility in dealing with access to sensitive information and information systems belonging to or being used on behalf of VA. To satisfy VA requirements, procedures defined in VA Directive & Handbook 0710, Personnel Suitability and Security Program are required.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that if security requirements for accessing, handling, and storing VA data and systems are specified in the contract, the contractor will comply with the contractual security requirements. The changed text removes this conditionality, simply stating that the contractor will handle the VA property. This creates a contradiction because the contradicted text assumes contractor personnel performing work under the contract shall satisfy all requirements for appropriate security eligibility, which is now uncertain. The change causes a direct conflict, as the changed text no longer explicitly mandates compliance with security requirements, leading to potential inconsistency in enforcement, as described in the 'Omissions' definition for in-text contradictions.

perturbation.original_text: The contractor may not use or disclose it except as the contractor is permitted to use or disclose VA sensitive information under the contract and this nondisclosure agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The contractor may not use or disclose it under this nondisclosure agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause indicating that the contractor can use or disclose information as permitted by the contract, it creates a contradiction. Other clauses reference permissions outlined elsewhere in the contract. Without that permission clause in this section, the contractor has a limited disclosure agreement, which creates an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The contractor may disclose VA sensitive information to persons other than in the performance of this contract as authorized by the contract and this agreement in only two situations: (1) pursuant to an order of a court of competent jurisdiction; or (2) with VA's prior written authorization. Prior to any disclosure pursuant to a court order, the contractor shall promptly notify VA of the court order and provide VA with a copy by fax or e-mail, whichever is faster, and notify by telephone the VA individual designated in advance to receive such notices. If the contractor cannot notify VA before being compelled to produce the information under court order, the contractor will notify VA of the disclosure as soon as practical. The notice under this provision will include the following information to the extent that the contractor knows it, if it does not show on the face of the court order: the records disclosed pursuant to the order, to whom, where, and when, for what purpose, and any other information that the contractor reasonably believes is relevant to the disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed the contractor to use or disclose VA sensitive information as permitted by the contract. The changed text removes this permission, stating the contractor may not use or disclose information 'under this nondisclosure agreement'. The contradicted text explicitly states the contractor may disclose VA sensitive information as authorized by the contract and agreement. Thus, the changed text creates a direct contradiction by eliminating a permission that the contradicted text relies upon, and the explanation given confirms this is a strong in-text contradiction as it limits the disclosure agreement to an extent which would not be legally aligned with the permissions outlined elsewhere in the contract.

perturbation.original_text: The contractor will also cooperate with VA in any civil litigation to recover VA property, to obtain monetary or other compensation for a third party, or to obtain injunctive relief against any third party who accessed, modified, disclosed, or destroyed VA sensitive data and computer systems except as authorized under the contract or this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The contractor will also cooperate with VA in any civil litigation.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception 'except as authorized under the contract or this agreement' regarding civil litigation, the clause contradicts the entire purpose of the NDA, which allows for specific authorized disclosures and actions. This change suggests the contractor must cooperate even if the actions in question were pre-approved.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The contractor may disclose VA sensitive information to persons other than in the performance of this contract as authorized by the contract and this agreement in only two situations: (1) pursuant to an order of a court of competent jurisdiction; or (2) with VA's prior written authorization. Prior to any disclosure pursuant to a court order, the contractor shall promptly notify VA of the court order and provide VA with a copy by fax or e-mail, whichever is faster, and notify by telephone the VA individual designated in advance to receive such notices. If the contractor cannot notify VA before being compelled to produce the information under court order, the contractor will notify VA of the disclosure as soon as practical. The notice under this provision will include the following information to the extent that the contractor knows it, if it does not show on the face of the court order: the records disclosed pursuant to the order, to whom, where, and when, for what purpose, and any other information that the contractor reasonably believes is relevant to the disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed the contractor to cooperate with VA in civil litigation, except when authorized under the contract. The changed text removes this exception, meaning the contractor must cooperate even if the actions were authorized. The contradicted text states the contractor may disclose VA sensitive information as authorized by the contract. The changed text creates a contradiction because the contractor must now cooperate in litigation even for authorized actions, conflicting with their right to disclose as authorized by contract.

perturbation.original_text: The contractor will not retain any copies of any of these documents.
perturbation.changed_text: The contractor may retain copies of these documents.
perturbation.explanation: By changing from 'will not retain' to 'may retain', it is creating a contradiction. VA requires data to be safely kept and the clause makes the data vunerable and exposed to the possiblity of VA confidential information being leaked.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The contractor and its employees shall not make any copies of any VA information, including software or applications that are not copyrighted, except as necessary to restore VA computer systems or data storage devices to an operating state. Any copies made by the contractor or subcontractor shall be identified as VA property and handled as sensitive information under this non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated the contractor 'will not retain any copies,' while the changed text states the contractor 'may retain copies.' This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text,' which states that the contractor 'shall not make any copies of any VA information...except as necessary to restore VA computer systems.' The changed text introduces a contradiction by allowing retention of copies, while the contradicted text severely restricts the making of copies in the first place, except for a very specific reason. This meets the definition of an Omissions-In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All costs associated with obtaining clearances for contractor provided personnel will be the responsibility of the contractor. Further, the contractor will be responsible for the actions of all individuals provided to work for VA under this contract. In the event that damages arise from work performed by contractor provided personnel, under the auspices of this contract, the contractor will be responsible for all resources necessary to remedy the incident.
perturbation.changed_text: Further, the contractor will be responsible for the actions of all individuals provided to work for VA under this contract.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the first and last sentences, the contract lacks explicit financial liability for security clearances and remediation. The removal of 'All costs associated with obtaining clearances for contractor provided personnel will be the responsibility of the contractor' contradicts the underlying security requirements outlined in section 4.a, creating an enforcement ambiguity around security costs.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Contractor personnel performing work under this contract shall satisfy all requirements for appropriate security eligibility in dealing with access to sensitive information and information systems belonging to or being used on behalf of VA. To satisfy VA requirements, procedures defined in VA Directive & Handbook 0710, Personnel Suitability and Security Program are required.
perturbation.justification: The original text clearly states the contractor's responsibility for costs associated with clearances and remediation, which is removed in the changed text. This omission contradicts the requirement in the 'Contradicted Text' that contractor personnel satisfy all security eligibility requirements, creating an enforcement ambiguity and contradicting the financial responsibility for meeting those security requirements. The removal of the explicit cost responsibility for security clearances directly conflicts with the expectation that the contractor will ensure personnel meet security standards, making it unclear who bears the financial burden of this requirement.

perturbation.original_text: Federal Acquisition Regulation clause 52.246-18, Warranty of Supplies of a Complex Nature (May 2001), is hereby incorporated into VA Form 0752. (a) Definitions. As used in this clause - "Acceptance" means the act of an authorized representative of the Government by which the Government assumes for itself, or as an agent of another, ownership of existing and identified supplies, or approves specific services rendered, as partial or complete performance of the contract. "Supplies" means the end items furnished by the Contractor and related services required under this contract. The word does not include "data." (b) Contractor's obligations. (1) The Contractor warrants that for two years all supplies furnished under this contract will be free from defects in material and workmanship and will conform with all requirements of this contract; provided, however, that with respect to Government-furnished property, the Contractor's warranty shall extend only to its proper installation, unless the Contractor performs some modification or other work on the property, in which case the Contractor's warranty shall extend to the modification or other work.
perturbation.changed_text: Federal Acquisition Regulation clause 52.246-18, Warranty of Supplies of a Complex Nature (May 2001), is hereby incorporated into VA Form 0752.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting all of the language of 52.246-18 except the opening line, we are removing the definition of 'Acceptance', 'Supplies', the Contractors Obligations, which all relate to the term and expectation of 'Warranty'. This act then contradicts Section 5, Software Program Integrity, which then is at odds, because of the fact the Contractor no longer has any Warranty obligations due to the definition and obligations that were omitted.
perturbation.contradicted_text: SOFTWARE PROGRAM INTEGRITY The contractor warrants and represents that the contractor-supplied software, other than the key software, does not and will not contain any program routine, device, code, or instructions (including any code or instructions provided by third parties) or other undisclosed feature, including, without limitation, a time bomb, virus, software lock, drop-dead device, malicious logic, worm, Trojan horse, bug, error, defect, or trap door, that is capable of accessing, modifying, deleting, damaging, disabling, deactivating, interfering with or otherwise harming the contractor software, any computers, networks, data, or other electronically stored information, or computer programs or systems (collectively, "disabling procedures"). Such representation and warranty applies regardless of whether such disabling procedures are authorized by the contractor to be included in the contractor software.
perturbation.justification: The original text incorporates FAR clause 52.246-18, including definitions and contractor obligations related to warranty. The changed text omits all of the FAR clause 52.246-18, except the opening line, removing the definition of 'Acceptance', 'Supplies', the Contractors Obligations. The contradicted text, 'SOFTWARE PROGRAM INTEGRITY', includes a warranty from the contractor regarding software defects. Because the changed text removes the contractor's warranty obligations by removing the entire clause that establishes this obligation, including definitions related to the term Warranty, it directly contradicts the software program integrity warranty elsewhere in the contract. This is an 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' as essential language is removed which creates a contradiction with obligations in other sections.
